2001-07-01.title: Nations in Asia Give English Their Own Flavorful Quirks
2001-07-01.abstract: Article on quirky, unique local expressions that flavor English language as it is spoken in various Asian nations as people adapt their own tongues to world's dominant second language; Singapore's Singlish quoted as example; photo; map; 350 million people around world are native English speakers, but more than one billion, mostly in Asia, are estimated to speak at least some English as well (M)

2001-07-01.title: Trade Panel Wants Global Economy's Riches Available to Poor Nations Too
2001-07-01.abstract: UNITED NATIONS, June 29 — A panel of financial and development experts has called for a new round of international trade talks devoted primarily to helping countries too weak to reap any benefits from the growing global economy.

2001-07-01.title: No Headline
2001-07-01.abstract: A panel of the world's leading financial and development experts called today for a new round of international trade talks devoted primarily to aiding the poor countries too weak to reap any benefits from the growing global economy.    The panel -- which included Robert Rubin, the former United States treasury secretary, and Ernesto Zedillo, the former president of Mexico - recommended that a ministerial-level World Trade Organization meeting planned for Qatar in November "should set an objective of making trade as free between industrial and developing countries as it already is among the industrial countries."

2001-07-01.title: Long-Range Justice Raises Fears for Sovereignty
2001-07-01.abstract: Arm of international law is growing longer for national leaders, such as Slobodan Milosevic, who are accused of atrocities; trend is celebrated by human rights advocates, but is viewed by some lawyers and governments as alarmingchallenge to national sovereignty and potentially unpredictable political tool subject to political abuse (M)

2001-07-01.title: Poverty Forces New Methods For Educating Turkish Youth
2001-07-01.abstract: Turkey, with one of world's youngest population and huge economic potential, is held back by deficiencies in centralized education system that leaves many without skills, particularly in rural areas where villages often have no school at all; only one student in five attends school beyond age 14; former bank executive Ibrahim Betil has responded with Education Volunteers Foundation that offers free classes in poor neighborhoods, runs education centers and mobile classrooms and introduces students to modern science and computer courses (M)

2001-07-02.title: Paris Stores Expand To Lure New Clientele
2001-07-02.abstract: PARIS—  With men ever more conscious about their clothes and their appearance, department stores here, eager to get in on the action, are refurbishing and expanding their men's stores.

2001-07-02.title: Roy Longmore Dies; Australian 'Legend,' 107
2001-07-02.abstract: Roy Longmore, who was proclaimed a legendary figure by the Australian government last year as one of the nation's last surviving veterans of the World War I battle at Gallipoli, died on June 21 at a nursing home in Melbourne. He was 107.    Mr. Longmore was a lowly enlisted man and he performed no exceedingly heroic feats. But he symbolized the sacrifices of the Anzacs, the 68,000 soldiers of the Australian and New Zealand Army Corps who fought in the gruesome and failed eight-month campaign to capture the Gallipoli peninsula from the Turks as a prelude to an attack on Constantinople, and he was granted a state funeral on Friday.

2001-07-02.title: Saudis Say They, Not U.S., Will Try 11 in '96 Bombing
2001-07-02.abstract: Saudi Arabia's interior minister said today that 11 of the 13 Saudis indicted by the United States about 10 days ago in connection with the truck bombing of the Khobar Towers apartments in 1996 were in prison in the kingdom and that the case would be referred to Saudi, but not American, courts soon.    The minister, Prince Nayef bin Abdel Aziz, when asked if any of the suspects held by Saudi Arabia would be sent to the United States to stand trial, responded: ''No. Never. Impossible.''

2001-07-02.title: Rulings Represent the First Check on Mahathir Regime in a Decade : Judicial Bravery Startles Malaysia
2001-07-02.abstract: KUALA LUMPUR—  A series of high-profile court rulings against the government recently has surprised lawyers in Malaysia and has provided the first significant legal check on the administration of Prime Minister Mahathir bin Mohamad in more than a decade.

2001-07-02.title: A Text Message That Simply Spells Romance
2001-07-02.abstract: PARIS—  Like a text message between two young lovers — Romeo and Juliet with cell phones — the words were printed on a peach-colored shirt, white Levis or flowered pants: "You break my heart;" "You make me happy." Or, on a black T-shirt, worn with plaid pants and sneakers, the universal message of "love, love, love."

2001-07-03.title: World Briefing | Asia: Japan: U.S. Told To Turn Over Serviceman
2001-07-03.abstract: Deputy Foreign Minister Yutaka Kawashima summoned the American chargé d'affaires, Richard Christianson, and demanded that the United States hand over an Air Force sergeant suspected of raping a Japanese woman in Okinawa last week. The authorities said the victim had identified Sgt. Timothy B. Woodland, 24, who is stationed at Kadena Air Base, as her attacker and that they had found his and her fingerprints on a car parked in a lot where the incident occurred. An Okinawa court issued a warrant for his arrest.   Calvin Sims (NYT)

2001-07-03.title: International Ulster Panel Blames I.R.A. for Unresolved Issue of Disarmament
2001-07-03.abstract: The international commission responsible for disarming Northern Ireland's paramilitary groups issued a bluntly worded report today saying the Irish Republican Army had taken no steps to dismantle its arsenal and would not indicate how it planned to do so.    The panel, headed by a retired Canadian general, John de Chastelain, reported that it had held a number of ''lengthy meetings'' with I.R.A. representatives since March and that it accepted as a commitment ''made in good faith'' the repeated pledge by the Catholic guerrilla group to put its arms ''beyond use, completely and verifiably.''

2001-07-03.title: World Briefing | Africa: Aid From West Drops Sharply
2001-07-03.abstract: Aid to Africa from rich Western nations fell by a third between 1994 and 1999, a United Nations report found. At the same time, investment in Africa stagnated. Africa still attracts less than 5 percent of all investment in the developing world at a time when it faces its severest health crisis with AIDS and suffers from low prices of commodities produced by many of its countries. The report was prepared for the United Nations Economic and Social Council, now meeting in Geneva.   Barbara Crossette (NYT)

2001-07-03.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Israel: City Doesn't Want Informers
2001-07-03.abstract: The mixed Jewish-Arab city of Akko, also known as Acre, petitioned the Supreme Court to stop the Shin Bet security agency from resettling any more Palestinian informers in their northern Israeli coastal city. Mayor Shmaryahu Biran said ''there is a consensus in Akko not to settle collaborators'' and not to become a ''collaborators' haven.''      Deborah Sontag (NYT)

2001-07-03.title: Algeria Warns Berber Protesters
2001-07-03.abstract: ALGIERS, July 2 (Reuters) — Prime Minister Ali Benflis issued a warning today to Berber delegates planning to march here, repeating that demonstrations were banned in the capital.

2001-07-04.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zimbabwe: Unions Call Two-Day Strike
2001-07-04.abstract: Zimbabwe workers protesting increase in gasoli

2001-07-04.title: Pelotas Journal; Of Gays and Gauchos (and Brazilian Gaucherie)
2001-07-04.abstract: Residents of Brazilian city of Pelotas are victims of peculiar prejudice: for more than century, Brazilians of every class and in every part of country have believed that men there are all homosexuals; city is treated as something of national joke, and to make matters worse, it is in state of Rio Grande do Sul, famous for its rough-riding cowboys and rugged, hyper-masculine image; historians trace bias to early 19th century, when Pelotas emerged as prosperous export center and newly wealthy families sent their sons off to be educated in France, where they acquired manners, habits and dress styles that caused them to be regarded at home as dandified intellectuals; annual carnival with long tradition for men to dress up as women has only reinforced city's image; map; photos (Pelotas Journal) (M)

2001-07-04.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: A Job For All The King's Men
2001-07-04.abstract: Final pieces of Navy surveillance plane that collided with Chinese fighter on Apr 1 are flown out of China on giant cargo plane to US, where engineers will determine whether aircraft can be put back together and flown again (S)

2001-07-04.title: Albanians Say Trying Top Leaders Isn't Enough
2001-07-04.abstract: Many Kosovo Albanians say trial of Slobodan Milosevic before international criminal tribunal should only be start of process to hold Serbs accountable for atrocities committed in Kosovo; photos (M)

2001-07-04.title: As Changeable as the Weather
2001-07-04.abstract: News analysis of Japanese Prime Min Junichiro Koizumi's shifting and contradictory positions on global warming as he travels from country to country on his first official overseas trip; says his decision-making process seems to be subordinating strictly environmental considerations to single issue on which he has staked his political fortunes: economic reform (M)

2001-07-05.title: World Briefing | Europe: Northern Ireland: Catholic Man Shot To Death
2001-07-05.abstract: Red Hand Defenders, breakaway Protestant paramilitary group, claims responsibility for killing of Ciaran Cummings, 19-year-old Roman Catholic man in Antrim; says shooting was retribution after local election gave two council seats to Sinn Fein, political wing of Irish Republican Army (S)

2001-07-05.title: World Briefing | Asia: India: Plans To Free 400 Civilians
2001-07-05.abstract: India says it will release 400 Pakistani civilian prisoners in move to improve 'people to people' ties with Pakistan and set positive tone for upcoming summit (S)

2001-07-05.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: North Korean's Trip Barred
2001-07-05.abstract: Hwang Jong Yop, top North Korean to defect to South, is denied permission to testify before US Congress; official cite concerns for his safety (S)

2001-07-05.title: Oshima Journal; After 90 Years, Small Gestures of Joy for Lepers
2001-07-05.abstract: Japanese government has formally apologized and paid damages for its past treatment of lepers; 4,500 people with leprosy who survive in Japan have been forced to live in isolation for decades, long after effective treatment had been found; court ruled in May that government must pay $15 million to 127 plaintiffs who challenged law that kept them confined to sanitariums on distant mountains and small islands like Oshima; photo; map (M)

2001-07-05.title: Taking a Breather on the Mideast
2001-07-05.abstract: Analysis of Bush administration's reluctant involvement in Israeli-Palestinian conflict; says Secretary of State Colin Powell only agreed to visit area after receiving highly unusual cable from American ambassadors in Israel, Egypt and Jordan, as well as consul general in Jerusalem, appealing to him to come to Israel and Palestinian territories day after bombing of Israeli disco in Tel Aviv; says Powell's visit was not viewed as success by Israel or by friendly Arab countries that Bush administraion tried to please by Powell visit; says it is unlikely that administration will dispatch any more high-profile missions in near future (M)

2001-07-06.title: Argentina in Quandary as Protest by Jobless Gets Nasty
2001-07-06.abstract: Group of 200 unemployed workers occupy central plaza of General Mosconi, Argentina, demanding jobs; government says protest represents threat to nation's political and social stability; group clashed with federal border police two weeks ago, leaving two bystanders dead and more than 40 others, including police officers, wounded; intelligence agencies link protestors to outside agitators, namely, Colombia's rebels or international narcotics traffickers trying to stir up trouble in part of Argentina; say they represent core of potential guerrilla insurgency; government of Pres Fernando de la Rua is divided over whether to negotiate with them or repress them, leading to embarrassing public feud between two cabinet ministers that has reinforced impression that government is adrift in its handling of three-year-old recession and rising unemployment; photos; map (M)

2001-07-06.title: World Briefing | Europe: Northern Ireland: Summit Meeting Next Week
2001-07-06.abstract: Prime Mins Blair of Britain and Bertie Ahern of Ireland will hold summit meeting with Northern Ireland political leaders to push for resolution of impasse over IRA disarmament (S)

2001-07-06.title: BUSH CALLS JIANG, VOICING CONCERNS OVER DETENTIONS
2001-07-06.abstract: Pres Bush telephones Pres Jiang Zemin of China, voicing concern about recent arrests of American citizens and green-card holders by Chinese security forces; senior official says Bush's overall message is need to build better relationship between two nations; it is unclear how tightly he linked future of that relationship to release of arrested scholars, journalists and businessmen; aides say Bush is under growing pressure in Congress to resolve incidents quickly; say Jiang offered noncommittal response on detainees, repeating China's oft-stated public position that cases would be 'expedited' and dealt with under Chinese law; Secretary of State Colin Powell plans to visit Beijing this month, and Bush is scheduled to go to China in October (M)

2001-07-06.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: No Risk From Animal Pyres
2001-07-06.abstract: British Food Standards Agency says levels of dioxin from animal pyres do not pose health hazards; almost 3.5 million animals were killed to halt foot-and-mouth disease (S)

2001-07-06.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iraq: Hussein Replaces Top Aide
2001-07-06.abstract: Iraqi Pres Saddam Hussein replaces deputy prime minister, naming Ahmed Hussein Khudayyir to post (S)

2001-07-07.title: A Bitter Scent of Loss for Mexican Party
2001-07-07.abstract: Institutional Revolutionary Party of Mexico saw mayor's race in Ciudad Juarez as chance for new start after last year's devastating presidential election; its candidate, Roberto Barraza, hoped to capitalize on wavering in Pres Vicente Fox's popularity; but Barraza has lost to Jesus Alfredo Delgado, member of Fox's PAN party, by landslide; Barraza says he will formally charge PAN with election tampering and seek to have results overturned, but there are worries that his apparent defeat could be final gasp for PRI; photo; map (M)

2001-07-07.title: World Briefing | Africa: Rwanda: Former Allies Hold Talks
2001-07-07.abstract: Pres Paul Kagame of Rwanda and Pres Yoweri Museveni of Uganda, former allies, meet near common border, vowing to improve relations that became strained after two nations invaded Congo in 1998; photo (S)

2001-07-07.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iraq: Report On Defectors To U.S.
2001-07-07.abstract: Press service run by Iraqi exiles says one of two Iraqi diplomats who reportedly defected in New York may have files on cases of Kuwaitis still missing after Iraqi invasion and occupation of Kuwait in 1990 (S)

2001-07-07.title: Fighting AIDS: A New War Is Killing Cambodians
2001-07-07.abstract: Cambodia, just as it is emerging from three decades of war and mass killings, finds itself swept by beginnings of new scourge: AIDS; Cambodia is sickest country in Asia now, at risk for a devastating spread of disease; Cambodia is seen at epicenter of next potential explosion of AIDS--Indochinese countries of Cambodia and Vietnam, as well as nearby Myanmar; photos; chart (L)

2001-07-08.title: Swiss Trial On Smuggling Shows Tension With Europe
2001-07-08.abstract: The trial of a reputed Italian Mafia boss suspected by Italian authorities of major cigarette smuggling has brought to the surface tensions between Switzerland and its European Union neighbors over contraband.    In the European Union view, Switzerland, which considers smuggling a customs violation but not a crime, has become a safe harbor for smugglers, who hide here while running hugely profitable businesses that siphon billions in tax dollars from other governments.

2001-07-08.title: Army Builds Defenses for Ulster Parade Today
2001-07-08.abstract: British Army begins its annual project of constructing metal and barbed-wire blockade in Portadown, Northern Ireland, to prevent parade by Protestant Orange Order from marching down road through Roman Catholic neighborhood; order has been banned from road, which it calls its traditional route, for last four years, to prevent outbreaks of violence between Protestants and Roman Catholics; photo (S)

2001-07-08.title: The Irish and Europe: A Time for Soul-Searching
2001-07-08.abstract: Irish voters sort out their reactions to their rejection of European Union treaty that would have paved way for 12 new members; from international point of view, Ireland seems to be turning cold shoulder toward applicant countries and denying them sort of help Irish used to escape poverty over last few decades; Irish reject that interpretation, saying process of European integration is increasingly bureaucratic and undemocratic; say institutions like European Central Bank and single European currency threaten Irish sovereignty; second national referendum on treaty will be held, probably next year (M)

2001-07-08.title: Agony Over Kashmir Echoes in Indian Courtroom
2001-07-08.abstract: Indian Army major being court-martialed for refusing order claims he is scapegoat for generals who escaped charges for one of country's worst military debacles; at issue is order Maj Manish Bhatnagar was given to attack Pakistani troops inside Indian territory two years ago, after Pakistani Army infiltrators penetrated along 15-mile front in Kargil area of Kashmir; Indian troops prevailed, but only after more than 850 of them died, as well as 700 Pakistani soldiers; Indian press accounts tell of ignored intelligence reports, and of soldiers being sent into battle without proper firearms or clothing; only two other officers involved in campaign, both majors, have faced courts-martial, with one winning acquittal and other being convicted of lesser offense; court-martial comes at sensitive time, as Indian Prime Min Atal Behari Vajpayee plans meeting with Gen Pervez Musharraf of Pakistan to talk about Kashmir; photo; map (M)

2001-07-08.title: Exasperated Arafat Says U.S. Should Do More
2001-07-08.abstract: Palestinian officials in Ramallah believe that Secretary of State Colin L Powell's recent trip to Middle East made no impact whatsoever on deteriorating situation and that far stronger American intervention is needed; Yasir Arafat himself, in rare interview with American newspaper, says he has sent letter to Pres Bush stating his concern that Americans have allowed Israelis and not neutral party to be judge of two sides' efforts to restore calm; Martin Indyk, departing American ambassador to Israel, says Arafat has not yet made '100 percent effort' to stop Palestinian violence against Israel of which he boasts; Terje Roed-Larsen, United Nations envoy, says Arafat is incapable of gaining complete control of chaotic situation and bringing violence to abrupt halt; says neither side should set standard that other cannot meet; photo (M)

2001-07-09.title: Patriarch Maximus V, 93; Led Greek Catholic Church
2001-07-09.abstract: Patriarch Maximus V Hakim, who for more than 30 years was spiritual leader of small Greek Catholic Church and who sought better relations with larger Eastern Orthodox Church, dies at age 93; photo (M)

2001-07-09.title: Ex-Ruling Party Challenges Result in Juárez Vote
2001-07-09.abstract: Institutional Revolutionary Party, pressing fight for political control of Juarez, Mexico's largest city on northern border, files complaint to have recent mayoral election overturned; candidate Roberto Barraza accuses incumbent Gustavo Elizondo of violating campaign laws that prohibit candidates and elected officials from making any public comments about elections within five days of vote (S)

2001-07-09.title: Army and Police Keep Order at Protestant March in Ulster
2001-07-09.abstract: British troops and police officers in riot gear mount huge security presence in Portadown, Northern Ireland, most volatile of all Ulster towns, and marching Orangemen limit their procession from Drumcree Church to token protest; extreme efforts by authorities to prevent Catholic-Protestant confrontation at annual parade, on eve of last-ditch talks to rescue faltering peace accord, discussed; photo (M)

2001-07-09.title: Medical Journals Plan to Offer Far Lower Rates in Poor Nations
2001-07-09.abstract: Six major publishers of medical journals will announce plans to give researchers in developing countries online access to their publications free or at sharply discounted prices; agreement covers about 1,000 of world's 1,240 medical journals; publishers include Blackwell Science, Elsevier Science, Harcourt Worldwide STM Group, Lippincott Williams & Wilkins, Springer-Verlag, John Wiley & Sons (S)

2001-07-09.title: But for Givenchy, No Lightning Bolts Strike : Dior's Fashion Thunder Steals Season Opening
2001-07-09.abstract: PARIS—  In France's storm-tossed weekend, John Galliano stole the fashion thunder with a magnificent and romantic show for Dior — flooded with color and with lightning bolts of imagination. Rajasthan gypsies, Wall Street pinstripes, Arab nomads and Tibetan folklore were melded with refinement and joie de vivre.

2001-07-10.title: African Migrants Risk All on Passage to Spain
2001-07-10.abstract: Growing tide of migrants from African countries is desperatly trying to enter Europe by way of Spain across treacherous Strait of Gibraltar, route once used almost exclusively by Moroccans; 15,000 illegal immigrants were detained along Spanish coast in 2000, more than four times as year before, and 20 percent were from sub-Saharan Africa; Spanish government is taking strong measures to seal southern coast, including installation of $120 million radar system to form electronic wall across strait; illegal immigration and its dangers have become troubling issues for Europe after year of calamitously failed attempts at immigrant smuggling; maps; photos (M)

2001-07-10.title: Fox Urged to Uphold Pledge on Justice Reforms
2001-07-10.abstract: Amnesty International urges Pres Vicente Fox to uphold pledge to reform Mexico's criminal justice system, in which allegations of torture are almost never properly investigated, and those responsible virtually never brought to justice (M)

2001-07-10.title: Effort by U.N. To Cut Traffic In Arms Meets A U.S. Rebuff
2001-07-10.abstract: Bush administration, which has already pulled away from range of international treaties, warns it will not join pact to curtail international flow of illegal small arms if it infringes on American right to own guns; John R Bolton, secretary of state for arms control and international security affairs, invokes Second Amendment at UN conference on trafficking in weapons; says US believes responsible use of firearms is legitimate aspect of national life; bluntness of remarks takes some delegates by surprise, even though American reluctance to expand gun controls internationally is shared by other countries, including China, India and Russia; two-week conference has set off angry responses in US among those opposed to gun control (M)

2001-07-10.title: At Last, Ivanisevic Catches Up to Wimbledon Destiny
2001-07-10.abstract: LONDON—  Destiny? Who believes in destiny anymore? But as the aces and the victories started to pile up during the last two weeks at Wimbledon, Goran Ivanisevic, for one and for once, certainly began to believe that it was finally meant to be.

2001-07-10.title: CHILE COURT BARS TRIAL OF PINOCHET
2001-07-10.abstract: Chilean appeals court rules Gen Augusto Pinochet, former dictator, is too ill to stand trial on charges of covering up killings of political opponents by death squad shortly before he seized power in 1973; concludes health problems have contributed to dementia so severe that he could not defend himself in court; human rights lawyers plan appeal; ruling confirms growing sense among legal scholars that prosecution was losing momentum because of appeals and quiet political pressures from civilian and military officials in last several months; many say Pinochet will almost certainly be spared trial on other suspected human rights violations if ruling stands; photos (M)

2001-07-11.title: Israel Bulldozes Houses in a Refugee Camp in the Gaza Strip
2001-07-11.abstract: Israeli Army bulldozers level houses and shops in Palestinian refugee camp in Rafah in Gaza Strip, saying buildings were used by gunmen as cover for attacks on troops; action touches off gun battle; US State Department and European Union criticize demolitions as complicating efforts to end crisis (S)

2001-07-11.title: World Briefing | Europe: Northern Ireland: Sinn Fein Leader's Warning
2001-07-11.abstract: Gerry Adams, Sinn Fein president, says make-or-break talks aimed at achieving IRA disarmament would fail if they continue 'making all other issues secondary to the issue of IRA arms' (S)

2001-07-11.title: Ousted Philippines President Officially Charged With Plunder
2001-07-11.abstract: Former Pres Joseph Estrada of Philippines is arraigned on charges of plundering country; photo (S)

2001-07-11.title: Commander Says His Troops Committed Crimes in Chechnya
2001-07-11.abstract: The commander of Russian military forces in Chechnya said today that his troops had committed "widespread crimes" in two days of terror they inflicted on the civilians in two Chechen villages

2001-07-11.title: Russians Terrorize 2 Villages After Chechen Mines Kill 4
2001-07-11.abstract: Hundreds of Russian Interior Ministry troops backed by helicopter gunships sweep into Assinovskaya and second Chechen village after two mine blasts kill and injure Russian soldiers; witnesses describe how troops arrested 1,500 people, looted homes and subjected men to beatings and electric shocks in day of terror; photos; map (M)

2001-07-12.title: Facing Crisis, The President Of Sri Lanka Calls a Vote
2001-07-12.abstract: Political opponents of Pres Chandrika Kumaratunga condemn her suspension of Parliament; analysts say she hoped to avoid no-confidence vote she fears she would lose; she has called for vote on Aug 21 on vaguely worded referendum that asks Sri Lankans whether they think new Constitution is needed; she favors new Constitution that would give ethnic Tamil minority greater autonomy to rule themselves; she also wants constitutional provisions that legal experts say would make electoral system more favorable to larger political parties like her Sri Lanka Freedom Party (M)

2001-07-12.title: Toronto Journal; Shedding a Bit More Than Its Image
2001-07-12.abstract: Toronto, Ontario, has undergone change as it has become metropolitan center, leaving behind image of prudence and oh-so-proper English restraint; as it grows in worldliness, Toronto has been letting its hair down in many ways; it has North America's first talk-radio station for guys, hosts one of North America's largest gay pride parades, and is site of Internet newscast in which anchors appear nude; photo (M)

2001-07-12.title: World Briefing | Europe: Yugoslavia: Warning On Revising Borders
2001-07-12.abstract: Yugoslav Pres Vojislav Kostunica warns against any redrawing of borders in Yugoslavia; says revisions could increase instability in region and in particular harm neighboring Macedonia (S)

2001-07-12.title: World Briefing | Asia: North Korea, Vietnam: A Friend In Need
2001-07-12.abstract: Kim Yong Nam, North Korea's second in command, travels to Vietnam in search of aid he hopes Vietnamese will give to show gratitude for past favors (S)

2001-07-13.title: On Top in Bulgaria: New Premier Is the Old King
2001-07-13.abstract: Bulgaria's deposed king, Simeon II of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha, announces that he will accept position as prime minister; Simeon returned from exile three months ago to lead his fledgling political party to sweeping victory in national legislative elections in June; map; photos (M)

2001-07-13.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: Outrage Over Japanese Textbook
2001-07-13.abstract: South Korea cancels military and cultural exchanges with Japan after Japan again refuses to revise new textbooks said to gloss over wartime atrocities; Pres Kim Dae Jung urges Japan to follow German example and teach historical facts (S)

2001-07-13.title: Oil High on Agenda as Bush Meets Algerian Leader
2001-07-13.abstract: Pres Bush meets with Abdelaziz Bouteflika in first visit of an Algerian head of state to White House in 16 years; visit comes on heels of massive protests in Algeria challenging Bouteflika government over economic hardships and authoritarian rule; White House spokesman says two leaders discussed 'energy issues and United States investment in Algeria'; Bouteflika also meets separately with Vice Pres Dick Cheney, who as former chief executive of Halliburton Co is well versed in Algeria's oil trade (M)

2001-07-13.title: World Briefing | Asia: Sri Lanka: Parliament Urged To Reopen
2001-07-13.abstract: Sri Lankan opposition leaders urge House speaker Anura Bandaranaike to call legislature back in to session, defying Pres Chandrika Kumaratunga's suspension (S)

2001-07-13.title: World Briefing
2001-07-13.abstract: Russian President Vladimir V. Putin signed a law requiring that political parties have national membership

2001-07-14.title: From Beachside Mansions to Cold Jail
2001-07-14.abstract: Vladimiro L Montesinos, Peru's former spy chief, has been confined to small, spare, dim jail cell since his arrest in Venezuela; faces 160 investigations into allegations that he smuggled drugs, trafficked arms to largest guerrilla group in Colombia, laundered money and traded favors and bribes with Peru's most powerful politicians, executives and military leadership; investigators also probe exact nature of his relationship with former Pres Alberto K Fujimori and whether he has more money stashed abroad beyond $264 million in bank accounts that they have found; photo (M)

2001-07-14.title: A Summit Meeting of Old Foes: India and Pakistan
2001-07-14.abstract: Gen Pervez Musharraf, Pakistani leader, arrives in New Delhi for three-day meeting with Prime Min Atal Behari Vajpayee of India; no agenda has been set for summit meeting, and outcome is so uncertain that leaders have not scheduled joint news conference for final day; photo (M)

2001-07-14.title: Russia Negotiating to Help Impoverished Nation With Reactor Project : Burma Seeks Nuclear Research Plant
2001-07-14.abstract: RANGOON—  In a bid to join the world's atomic nations, Burma is negotiating to build a nuclear reactor with Russian financial and technical help.

2001-07-14.title: Farmland Battle Tests South African Leaders
2001-07-14.abstract: South African government wins praise from some businessmen, white farmers and politicians for demolishing shacks put up illegally on farms, but its resolve is being tested by haunting images of homeless women weeping before their ruined shacks and complaints from some blacks that government seems to care more about big landowners and investors than the poor; even critics acknowledge that South Africa needs approval of Western governments to win foreign investment so that economy can grow and government can make good on its promises of housing, land and electricity for poor (M)

2001-07-14.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: Anger At U.S. 'Slavery' Report
2001-07-14.abstract: South Korea's Foreign Ministry assails US State Department report accusing country of ignoring trafficking in women (S)

2001-07-15.title: Fighting Sex Harassment, and Stigma, in Japan
2001-07-15.abstract: Interview with female university student who successfully sued Osaka prefectural governor Knock Yokoyama in 1999 for sexually molesting her while she worked on his campaign; her continued need for anonymity is indicative of ostracism and loss many Japanese women fear if they protest groping and other sexual harassment too vigorously in society where male power and privilege are deeply rooted; photos (M)

2001-07-15.title: Ulster Talks Fail to Solve Problem of I.R.A. Weapons
2001-07-15.abstract: Five days of crisis talks aimed at rescuing failing Northern Ireland peace accord end without breakthrough in impasse over Irish Republican Army's refusal to give up arms; British Prime Min Tony Blair and Irish Prime Min Bertie Ahern plan new package of proposed solutions; cite progress (M)

2001-07-15.title: Front Line Of U.N. Effort To Take Guns From Children
2001-07-15.abstract: Gen Romeo Dallaire and other officals of new United Nations program discuss effort to stop trafficking in kind of guns most easily obtained by children, news conference; Jose Salema comments on running one of pioneering programs to persuade people of all ages to part with guns for jobs or community improvements (M)

2001-07-15.title: Beijing Convicts Professor; Orders Expulsion
2001-07-15.abstract: China convicts Prof Li Shaomin, Chinese-born American citizen, of spying for Taiwan; orders him expelled; move resolves first of several cases that have complicated US-China ties (M)

2001-07-15.title: Riots Point Out Jamaican Fault Line
2001-07-15.abstract: Residents of West Kingston, Jamaica, neighborhood that was site of recent fatal clash with police turn to opposition leader Edward Seaga, who represents area in Parliament, for food, medicine and reassurance; his party and ruling party both use community leaders to aid needy residents of areas loyal to them and to act as enforcers in turf wars that have long divided nation; photos (M)

2001-07-16.title: New Links in Khmer Rouge Chain of Death
2001-07-16.abstract: Newly discovered documents provide first concrete evidence of direct involvement of Khmer Rouge leaders in killings of nearly one million Cambodians and death of another million from disease and starvation during Khmer Rouge regime, between 1975 and 1979; open new window on leaders' responsibility, often covered in minute detail, in chain of command that led to massacres; documents are contained in report by War Crimes Research Office of American University College of Law; presents evidence against seven of most senior living Khmer Rouge leaders, including Nuon Chea, Ieng Sary and Khieu Samphan; photo; map (M)

2001-07-16.title: Israeli Cabinet Rules Out Idea Of Exchange Of Territory
2001-07-16.abstract: Israeli government formally buries idea of land exchanges with Palestinians, concept raised during Camp David peace talks year ago; rejection takes form of decision by Prime Min Ariel Sharon and cabinet to build several newtowns in Halutza Sands region, part of Negev near Gaza Strip and near border witn Egypt; Sharon's predecessor, Ehud Barak, proposed to Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat, that Palestinians take controlof Halutza Sands, which is part of Israel proper, in return for agreeing to Israel's annexation of parts of West Bank that contain heaviest concentration of Jewish settlers; map; photo (M)

2001-07-16.title: U.S. Envoy, A Bridge To Vietnam, Ends Tenure
2001-07-16.abstract: US Amb Pete Peterson ends four-year tenure as America's first postwar ambassador to Vietnam; was former prisoner of war and Democratic Congressman; his success in bringing aura of common sense to relationship still freighted with resentments and mistrust, earning respect in both Hanoi and Washington, discussed; photo (M)

2001-07-16.title: North Cypriot Editor Speaks His Mind
2001-07-16.abstract: Any attempt to understand north Cyprus is complicated by the emotive claims of the Greek Cypriots and by the uncanny ability of the Turkish Cypriot leader, Rauf Denktash, to skew any argument in his favor. Both his critics and his supporters admit Mr. Denktash's skills as a lawyer have been central to his durability as a politician.

2001-07-16.title: Shanghai Journal; High-Priced High-Rises Displace the Proletariat
2001-07-16.abstract: Heart of Shanghai, China, where Mao Zedong founded Communist Party 80 years ago, is being transformed by urban renewal project from teeming proletarian residential neighborhood into landscaped corporate park and luxury housing complex where private homes will sell for millions of dollars; photo (M)

2001-07-17.title: White House Unconcerned About China-Russia Accord
2001-07-17.abstract: Bush administration plays down importance of friendship pact between Moscow and Beijing, saying it represents short-term interests rather than long-term strategy that should concern US; says new treaty does not signify sudden blossoming of broad and deep relationship (M)

2001-07-17.title: World Briefing | Africa: South Africa: Hearings Start On Soccer Deaths
2001-07-17.abstract: South African commission of inquiry opens public hearings into stampede at soccer match in Johannesburg three months ago that killed 43 fans (S)

2001-07-17.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Saving The Caspian Sturgeon
2001-07-17.abstract: Russia, responding to warnings that stocks of sturgeon are perilously low in Caspian Sea, will suspend commercial fishing for sturgeon (S)

2001-07-17.title: Bush Forgoes Trying to Bar Cuba Deals By Foreigners
2001-07-17.abstract: Pres Bush, in setback for Cuban exile lobby and its Congressional allies, says he will not activate harshest sanction of 1996 Helms-Burton Act that seeks to punish foreigners for investing in American property in Cuba that was confiscated by Cuban government; says move is in US national interest and will expedite transition to democracy in Cuba; action appears aimed in part at avoiding conflict with European countries, critical of his policies and regard Helms-Burton as example of US legislating beyond its borders; decision raises likelihood that full force of Helms-Burton Act will never take effect; hard-line critics of Cuban government express dismay, but say Bush's approach to Cuba is preferable to Pres Clinton's more conciliatory stance (M)

2001-07-17.title: Russia and China Sign 'Friendship' Pact
2001-07-17.abstract: Russia and China sign treaty of friendship and cooperation with effusive gestures of camraderie in Kremlin; treaty, first such agreement signed since era of Stalin and Mao, binds them closer in next 20 years, and also commits them to oppose jointly much of framework for international security US is seeking to erect following cold war; joins both formally in opposing US missile plans and places Russia more firmly behind China's claim of sovereignty over Taiwan; strengthens military cooperation between Beijing and Moscow while rejecting intervention undertaken by NATO in Balkans in 1999 to stop killing of civilians; accord, sought by China bears markings of strategic pact that sets forth deep concerns shared by Moscow and Beijing about new world order dominated by US and European allies; Bush administration plays down its importance; says it falls far short of cementing alliance between two countries and comes as no surprise; photos (M)

2001-07-18.title: Protesters at Bay, Rich Nations' Chiefs to Meet in Genoa
2001-07-18.abstract: Tensions and security measures are high in Genoa in advance of summit of world's major industrial countries, which is also expected to draw more than 100,000 antiglobalization protesters; bomb scares and rigid security preparations described; photo; leaders will stay aboard cruise ship European Vision, except for Pres Bush, who will be at Jolly Hotel Marina (S)

2001-07-18.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: 47 Killed By Explosives
2001-07-18.abstract: Blast in northwest China caused by illegal explosives stored in a house kills 47 people and injures 85; illegal explosives sold to mines and quarries are cheaper than state-licensed products (S)

2001-07-18.title: No Failure at Talks
2001-07-18.abstract: India and Pakistan, longtime foes,  emerged from two days of talks without even a joint news release to show for it. Yet ministers from both countries said that the meeting had not failed, but had helped them better understand each other.    Article, Page A7.

2001-07-18.title: World Briefing | Asia: Afghanistan: No Dancing, No Surfing. (Surfing?)
2001-07-18.abstract: Taliban bans use of Internet in Afghanistan to curb un-Islamic influences (S)

2001-07-18.title: Mexico's New Anti-Drug Team Wins the Trust of U.S. Officials
2001-07-18.abstract: US and Mexican officials say that after five years of failure, they have created trusted group of undercover Mexican investigators who are arresting long-sought drug suspects and attacking drug cartels instead of selling out to them; photos; never-ending river of cocaine and heroin still flows north and drug barons are still using profits to corrupt Mexican law enforcement, but new 117-member unit is now working with DEA; Joseph Keefe of DEA says new unit, each member rigorously checked in both nations, is new way of doing business, with Americans more confident about sharing information; Jose Santiago Vasconcelos, who oversees special unit, agrees (M)

2001-07-19.title: Belgrade Journal; Rakish City of the Serbs, Fighting Back the Tears
2001-07-19.abstract: Former Serbian Pres Slobodan Milosevic was handed to war crimes tribunal in The Hague two weeks ago, but there is little sense in Belgrade that many people are in mourning for him; of more concern are pensions still largely unpaid, 40 percent unemployment and impact of hundreds of thousands of refugees from lost wars who loiter or hawk wares on streets; photo (M)

2001-07-19.title: Cash-for-Tickets Case Haunts Chirac as Report Raises the Figure
2001-07-19.abstract: New report finds Pres Jacques Chirac spent $360,000 on lavish trips getween 1992 and 1995, only three days after Chirac called reports of his spending $300,000 exaggerated; auditors from Justice Dept say they cannot be sure they found all of Chirac's purchases during time because many of invoices were under fake names; new figures are another setback for Chirac, who has been battling allegations of corruption for more than year and is expected to run for re-election next year (M)

2001-07-19.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: Pressure On Japan
2001-07-19.abstract: South Korea's National Assembly presses campaign against Japan's middle-school textbooks which critics say gloss over wartime atrocities by passing resolution asking government to block Japan's campaign for seat on UN Security Council (S)

2001-07-19.title: Fugitive Fujimori Relative is Shielded by Japan
2001-07-19.abstract: Japanese government says it granted citizenship to former Peruvian Pres Alberto Fujimori's brother-in-law, Victor Aritomi Shinto, who is wanted in Peru on suspicion of embezzling public funds; move effectively shields Aritomi from being sent back to Peru, since Japan does not extradite its citizens; Japan has already said it would not honor request to extradite Fujimori because he is Japanese citizen by ethnic heritage; legal scholars say both Fujimori and Aritomi are now legally protected from prosecution for crimes committed abroad as long as they remain in Japan (M)

2001-07-19.title: Few Missile Defense Details Emerge After Powell Talks
2001-07-19.abstract: Secretary of State Colin L Powell and Russian foreign minister, Igor S Ivanov, play guessing game over what each side intends on sensitive issue of missile defense; Pres Vladimir V Putin tells reporters he is open to idea of negotiating accord over limited missile defense without discarding decades of arms control agreements with US: Powell says it is time to give Russians fuller picture of what Bush administration intends on missile defense and how it wants to work around 1972 Antiballistic Missile Treaty(M)

2001-07-20.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Sister Sent To Labor Camp
2001-07-20.abstract: Li Wangling, sister of Chinse labor activist Li Wangyang, is charged with subversion and sentenced to three years in labor camp, apparently for talking with foreign reporters (S)

2001-07-20.title: U.S. at Odds With Europe Over Rules on World Drug Pricing
2001-07-20.abstract: Bush administration and European Union are at odds over how to spend $10 billion that United Naitons hopes to raise to fight AIDS, particularly on pharmaceutical drugs; at issue is creation of any system to regulate world drugs, which Bush administration and giant pharmaceutical companies opposes; administration contends that patent rights must be protected and wants companies left alone to offer discounts when they see fit; Europeans appear to side with poor countries and campaigners for cheaper drugs (M)

2001-07-20.title: Jeffrey Archer, Guilty of Perjury, Is Jailed for Four Years
2001-07-20.abstract: Jeffrey Archer, millionaire author and member of House of Lords who has had repeated brushes with scandal, is convicted on perjury charges and jailed for four years; jury also finds him guilty of perverting course of justice; Archer is found guilty of creating false diaries and concocting bogus story to win 1987 trial of his libel suit against newspaper for reporting that he slept with prostitute and paid her hush money; Daily Star, newspaper he sued, says it will seek $3 million from him in civil action based on finding that he lied and forged papers to win suit; photo (M)

2001-07-20.title: In a Skirmish Along Party Lines, Daschle Talks of Isolationism and Republicans Fire Back
2001-07-20.abstract: Partisan battle erupts over direction of Pres Bush's foreign policy, and propriety of criticizing him while he is overseas; at issue are remarks made by Tom Daschle, Senate majority leader, who during question-and-answer session with newspapers delivered wide-ranging critique of Bush's foreign policy, saying it is driving wedge between United States and its allies as well as eroding America's leadership position in international affairs; Bush, in Britain, dismisses charges of isolationism, but remarks set off firestorm of criticism from Republicans; Daschle stands by his criticism of Bush's policy but says he should have been more attuned to timing (M)

2001-07-20.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Prosecutors Hold Soldiers
2001-07-20.abstract: Russian prosecutors detain six soldiers in probe of alleged torture and looting in Chechen village (S)

2001-07-21.title: Protester Is Killed in Genoa; Leaders Upbeat on Economy : Violence Rages at G-8 Talks
2001-07-21.abstract: GENOA—  Rampaging protesters turned parts of Genoa into a war zone on Friday as the death of one man together with more than 100 injuries overshadowed the efforts by Group of Eight leaders to talk up the world economy.

2001-07-21.title: China and India Face Off in Nepal
2001-07-21.abstract: JAKARTA—  Only a present day William Shakespeare could imagine the real life tragedy in Nepal when the Crown Prince eliminated an entire line of a royal dynasty that had ruled that land for more than 200 years.

2001-07-21.title: An Israeli Hints At Permitting U.S. Observers
2001-07-21.abstract: Defense Min Binyamin Ben-Eliezer hints that Israel might agree to foreigners monitoring worsening conflict with Palestinians, but says outsiders would have to be Americans; other officials close to Prime Min Ariel Sharon seek to minimize importance of remarks, which come day after foreign ministers of major industrialized countries urged introduction of 'third-party' monitors as way to keep violence from spinning out of control, a suggestion immediately embraced by Palestinian leaders and rejected by Israelis; photo (M)

2001-07-21.title: World Briefing | Americas: Argentina: Chasing Pinochet, Again
2001-07-21.abstract: Federal Judge Rodolfo Canicoba Corral asks Chile to extradite former Chilean dictator Augusto Pinochet to Argentina so he can faces questions about conspiracy by South American dictators to execute leftists in 1970's and 1980's (S)

2001-07-21.title: ITALIAN PROTESTER IS KILLED BY POLICE AT GENOA MEETING
2001-07-21.abstract: Italian protester Carlo Giuliani is shot and killed by police officer during anti-globalization demonstration by several thousand protesters at Group of Eight economic summit in Genoa; victim is one of several young men who attack police van with rocks and other weapons; Italian Pres Carlo Azeglio Ciampi, appearing with Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi, expresses sorrow over death; Berlusconi suggests that venues for such high-level meetings be reconsidered; Vittorio Agnoletti, head of Genoa Social Forum, umbrella association of more than 700 groups protesting meeting, calls for immediate suspension of sessions; deplores violence by small group of protesters that most demonstrators does not condone; map; photos (M)

2001-07-22.title: Farm Unrest in Mexico Challenges New President
2001-07-22.abstract: Mexico's small farmers are being battered by explosion of free trade under Nafta, plummeting market prices and cuts in government support; they complain that free trade accord has not given them access to American markets they were promised, and has forced hundreds of them out of business; tens of thousands of farmers hold protests around country, including 5,000 sugar cane farmers who converge on capital and block access to government offices; for Mexico's new president, Vicente Fox, reforming agricultural industry and finding alternative employment for millions of subsistence farmers at time of widespread resentment may prove his most important domestic challenge; photos; map (L)

2001-07-22.title: Conservatives in Iran, Despite Defeat, Try to Thwart Reformers
2001-07-22.abstract: Iran's hardline conservatives appear to be intensifying their efforts to thwart reform movement, despite their overwhelming defeat in presidential elections last month; they are using institutions like judiciary, police and unelected religious organizations they control to place added pressure on reformers, who had hoped that Pres Mohammad Khatami's re-election with 78 percent of vote would lead to smoother second term; judiciary has made accusations against 10 reformist members of Parliament, and other reformers find themselves embroiled in scandals; political analysts say conservatives hope to discredit reform movment and, eventually, to frustrate Khatami's attempts at bringing greater political openness and economic development to Iran (M)

2001-07-22.title: European Papers Doubt Value of Summit Talks
2001-07-22.abstract: As tens of thousands of protesters continued to clash with the police outside the summit meeting of major industrial nations in Genoa, Italy, there was mounting opinion in Europe that such conferences should be dropped in the future

2001-07-22.title: ALLIES TELL BUSH THEY'LL ACT ALONE ON CLIMATE ACCORD
2001-07-22.abstract: United States' leading allies, at summit meeting in Genoa, tell Pres Bush they will move ahead and ratify Kyoto Protocol on global warming by next year, even without American participation; officials suggest that round of telephone calls among leaders of Japan, France and Germany has left Bush isolated on issue; Prime Min Jean Chretien of Canada says his nation also is ready to ratify treaty, and that negotiators will work to come up with common language about global warming for communique to be issued on July 22; says negotiators in Bonn, where meeting on subject is under way, would work on details of how Kyoto accord would be implemented; says Americans agreed to come up with alternative proposal to Kyoto, but had not promised date for delivering their plan; without American participation, treaty would be largely ineffective; in Genoa, as many as 50,000 demonstrators flow through streets, as protesters and riot police clash anew; photo (M)

2001-07-22.title: Efforts to Oust Its President Move Ahead In Indonesia
2001-07-22.abstract: Indonesia's legislature begins proceedings that could quickly put end to erratic and ineffectual 21-month-old tenure of Pres Abdurrahman Wahid; his removal would not address critical economic and social problems that countinue to overwhelm nation, world's fourth most populous; Wahid is country's first democratically elected leader in generation, but his unpredictable behavior, his acerbic tongue and his nonstop political game playing quickly eroded his support; he faces rejection from almost every corner, with 38-member military and police bloc joining legislative vote against him; if he is removed, he would be replaced by Vice Pres Megawati Sukarnoputri; she has been preparing herself to assume office, but she would inherit same daunting problems and same political infighting that dogged Wahid (M)

2001-07-23.title: Rebels Secure a Base in Macedonian Town
2001-07-23.abstract: Ethnic Albanian rebels take advantage of NATO-brokered cease-fire to move into Tetovo and few villages in mainly Albanian-populated area of western Macedonia, under noses of government forces; dozens of heavily armed Macedonian Albanian fighters occupy outer districts of Tetovo and stand just 300 yards from nearest police checkpoint; maps; photo (M)

2001-07-23.title: Buenos Aires Journal; Recession Blues Are Turning Into a Ballad of the Sad Cafes
2001-07-23.abstract: Bistros of Buenos Aires, which define city's social and cultural life, are dealt blow by Argentina's three-year-old recession; more and more people are staying home to save money; tributes to cafe life are as frequent in tango songs as heartfelt considerations of unrequited love; photo (M)

2001-07-23.title: Guide Proposed for Trials of Rogue Leaders
2001-07-23.abstract: Group of leading international legal scholars and jurists proposes first set of guidelines for trials of public figures on charges like genocide and crimes against humanity; guidelines, to be published by Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Affairs at Princetion University, are work of more than 30 legal experts from at least half dozen countries and several international orgainzations, including UN; Princeton Principles on Universal Jurisdiction are arranged as 14 provisions governing all aspects of international trials from definitions of crimes to dispute between governments over accused (M)

2001-07-23.title: Bush Firm on Missile Shield Despite Treaty Violation
2001-07-23.abstract: President Bush said today if he cannot reach an accord with President Vladimir Putin of Russia he will move ahead with testing missile shield technology

2001-07-23.title: Climate Talks Stuck
2001-07-23.abstract: For the second time in eight months, the fate of the first treaty to require cuts in gases linked to global warming came down to a marathon all-night session as countries sought to ensure that the agreement did not harm their economies or other interests.    Article, Page A3.

2001-07-24.title: China Sentences Three People to Jail Terms for Spying
2001-07-24.abstract: Two permanent United States residents were among those sentenced today for spying for Taiwan, which threatens to overshadow Secretary of State Colin L. Powell's visit later this week

2001-07-24.title: Bush Hears Pope Condemn Research in Human Embryos
2001-07-24.abstract: President Bush, facing a decision whether to allow federal financing for research using human embryo cells, heard an appeal today from Pope John Paul II to ''reject practices that devalue and violate human life at any stage from conception until natural death.''    The pope made his statement after meeting Mr. Bush at the papal summer residence here outside Rome, declaring that this path was the obligation of ''a free and virtuous society, which America aspires to be.'' He specifically declared that the creation of human embryos for research purposes, which some American scientists have begun doing, was an evil akin to euthanasia and infanticide. [Excerpts, Page A8.]

2001-07-24.title: British and French to Resume Concorde Flights
2001-07-24.abstract: British Airways will resume flights of its Concorde supersonic jet in September, and Air France will follow a month later, a coalition of French and British transport officials said today.    The group met in London today for the eighth time since an Air France Concorde crashed near Paris a year ago, killing 113.

2001-07-24.title: World Briefing | Asia: Nepal: Truce With Maoist Rebels
2001-07-24.abstract: The government and Maoist rebels said they would halt all offensive actions to pave the way for peace talks. The new prime minister, Sher Bahadur Deuba, took office on Sunday and appealed to the rebels to stop their violent uprising. He was chosen by the Nepalese Congress to replace Girija Prasad Koirala, who resigned last week under pressure from the opposition and his own party. The Maoists killed at least 17 policemen in a Sunday attack in Pandusen village in the western reaches of the country.      Barry Bearak (NYT)

2001-07-24.title: Woman in the News; A Daughter of Destiny; Megawati Sukarnoputri
2001-07-24.abstract: When she was still something of a neophyte six years ago, Megawati Sukarnoputri spoke with wonder about the hard work of politics. The most difficult thing to learn, she said, was patience.    ''It's only human that I have ups and downs,'' she said then. ''I've had to train myself to remain clear and detached in the face of problems.''

2001-07-25.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: Protests Over A Death At G-8 Summit
2001-07-25.abstract: Tens of thousands of people protest in peaceful marches in cities thorught Italy against use of police force that leaves one person dead and more than 230 injured at G-8 summit in Genoa; photo (S)

2001-07-25.title: China Riled Over Planned Visit to Japanese War Shrine
2001-07-25.abstract: A controversy over a planned prime ministerial visit next month to a Shinto shrine honoring Japan's war dead escalated sharply today with a stern warning by China to call off the ceremony

2001-07-25.title: Palestinians Angrily Protest Arrest of Militants
2001-07-25.abstract: Hundreds of Palestinians, angered at arrest of Hamas and Fatah militants by Palestinian Authority, hurl stones and fire shots at home of Gaza security chief Moussa Arafat; riot points up resistance Yasir Arafat faces in trying to rein in violence; 18-year-old Israeli, Yuri Gushchin, is found shot and stabbed to death near Ramallah (S)

2001-07-25.title: World Briefing | Africa: Mozambique: Convictions In Prison Deaths
2001-07-25.abstract: Mozambique court convicts two police officers for their part in deaths of at least 83 prisoners, most of them opposition supporters, who apparently suffocated last November soon after being locked up in small, poorly ventilated cell; crackdown last November came as supporters of Mozambican National Resistance, known as Renamo, clashed with police around country over ruling party's unwillingness to include Renamo in government (S)

2001-07-25.title: U.S. Explores Other Options On Preventing Germ Warfare
2001-07-25.abstract: Senior Bush administration official says that although White House rejects draft agreement under discussion at talks in Geneva, it remains committed to strengthening 1972 treaty banning biological weapons, and is exploring alternative approaches (S)

2001-07-26.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zimbabwe: Mugabe Reaffirms Land Transfer
2001-07-26.abstract: Pres Robert Mugabe of Zimbabwe says government will continue to redistribute white-owned farms to blacks, even though continued occupation by black squatters has been ruled illegal by courts (S)

2001-07-26.title: World Briefing | Middle East: West Bank: Hamas Activist Killed
2001-07-26.abstract: Israelis kill Salah Darwaza, Palestinian described as Islamic militant from Hamas who helped plan recent bombings in Israel; photo (S)

2001-07-26.title: World Briefing
2001-07-26.abstract: EUROPE THE HAGUE: CROAT REPORTS FOR TRIAL A Croatian Army general turned himself over to the United Nations war crimes tribunal to face charges linked to the killing of Serbian civilians during Serbian-Croatian fighting in 1993. Gen. Rahim Ademi surrendered after the tribunal's prosecutor pressured Croatia to arrest him and another Croat commander for trial. General Ademi said he was proud of his war record and wanted to clear his name. He is the top Croat among the tribunal's 40 detainees and joins former President Slobodan Milosevic of Yugoslavia and four other generals at the tribunal's cellblock.    Marlise Simons (NYT) GERMANY: WOE, THE ETERNAL STUDENT The right to study on and on at taxpayers' expense took a blow when a court upheld a state's right to charge tuition from students who overstay their welcome. When Baden- Württemberg created a fee of $448 per semester for a student who studies beyond seven years, four students went to court claiming violation of their right to choose an occupation freely. The German Federal Administrative Court said the state was justified. On average, a German student spends 13.5 semesters at college and finally leaves school at age 32, after breaks for travel, work or military service.    (AP) ITALY: G-8 PROTESTER MOURNED Nearly 1,000 mourners, including left-wing politicians, attended the funeral in Genoa of Carlo Giuliani, 23, the protester shot by a policeman last week during the G-8 summit meeting. His coffin was decorated with ferns and the flag of his favorite soccer team, Roma. He was the first person killed in antiglobalization protests that began in Seattle in 1999.    Alessandra Stanley (NYT) ITALY: ARROW KILLED THE ICEMAN The Bronze Age hunter whose 5,300-year-old frozen body was discovered in the Tyrolean Alps was shot through the chest with an arrow, according to the South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology in Bolzano. Scientists said the fatal flint arrowhead was firs

2001-07-26.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: G-8 Protester Mourned
2001-07-26.abstract: Nearly 1,000 mourners, including left-wing politicians, attend funeral of 23-year-old Carlo Giuliani, fatally shot by police during antiglobalization protests at G-8 economic summit in Genoa, Italy (S)

2001-07-26.title: U.S. Rejects New Accord Covering Germ Warfare
2001-07-26.abstract: United States rejects international accord aimed at enforcing 1972 treaty banning germ weapons but says it will continue looking for ways to salvage proposal; this appears to effectively scuttle attempt to strengthen original United Nations treaty that prohibits developing, producing or possessing biological weapons; protocol rejected by Bush administration is aimed at providing compliance provisions lacking in treaty; Donald A Mahley, chief negotiator for US, says draft protocol would put national security and confidential business information at risk; senior Bush administration official claims Clinton administation had same concerns (M)

2001-07-27.title: World Briefing | Asia: India: 'Bandit Queen' Is Cremated
2001-07-27.abstract: Phoolan Devi, India's 'bandit queen' who was shot to death, is cremated near Ganges River in home state of Uttar Pradesh; police launch hunt for killers (S)

2001-07-27.title: Freed Chinese Scholar, Still Defiant, Returns to the U.S.
2001-07-27.abstract: American-based Chinese scholar Gao Zhan returns to US after five months in Chinese jail, and promptly defies Chinese authorities who convicted her of spying and sentenced her to ten years in jail, before expelling her; sociologist at American University says she was warned not to talk about experience but will give account in print, although she sidesteps some sensitive questions on advice of husband, Xue Donghua, and Sen George F Allen of Virginia; photo with family at Dulles International Airport (M)

2001-07-27.title: Powell Presses to Resume Talks With North Korea
2001-07-27.abstract: Secretary of State Colin L. Powell said today that American officials were prepared to go anywhere at any time of North Korea's choice to resume talks

2001-07-27.title: Powell, in Hanoi, Pauses To Put the Past to Rest
2001-07-27.abstract: Sec Colin Powell's return to Vietnam, where he served during war, brings back flood of memories as he flies over once-hostile terrain and attends haunting memorial to seven Americans and nine Vietnamese who died in air crash this year while searching for missing Americans; photo (M)

2001-07-27.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: U.S. Ships Enter Hong Kong
2001-07-27.abstract: Two US warships sail into Hong Kong harbor in resumption of port calls, which were halted by Chinese government after midair collision between Chinese and American military planes over South China Sea in April; photo (S)

2001-07-28.title: World Briefing | Asia: North Korea: Drought Puts Thousands In Peril
2001-07-28.abstract: United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization and United Nations World Food Program say immediate food aid is needed by North Koreans, who race increasing starvation as result of another serious drought (S)

2001-07-28.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Invites India
2001-07-28.abstract: Following a gesture made at the recent Agra summit, the military ruler, Gen. Pervez Musharraf, has sent a formal invitation to India's prime minister, Atal Behari Vajpayee, to visit Pakistan. Mr. Vajpayee gave his informal acceptance when the idea was first presented. No dates have been announced, but Indian officials have said it is unlikely that such a trip could be made this year.   Barry Bearak (NYT)

2001-07-28.title: World Briefing | Asia: Cambodia: Repatriating Minority Refugees
2001-07-28.abstract: Cambodian government says it is preparing to send back to Vietnam more than 300 refugees who fled across border from Central Highlands during crackdown that followed protests earlier this year; announcement comes despite Vietnam's refusal to allow United Nations refugee agency access to region (S)/

2001-07-28.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: Berlin Wall's Long Shadow
2001-07-28.abstract: Three pieces of Berlin Wall are to be transported to United Nations headquarters in New York, where they will be put on display (S)

2001-07-28.title: World Briefing
2001-07-28.abstract: Scotish authorities began extradition proceedings against a resident of Edinburgh wanted in Lithuania to stand trial on charges of genocide

2001-07-29.title: American Embroiled in British Subway Debate
2001-07-29.abstract: Robert R Kiley, American mass transit expert hired by London's mayor to restore its crumbling subway system, is getting cold shoulder from Prime Min Tony Blair's government; it fired him from job it gave him just 10 weeks earlier, then obtained emergency court injunction to keep him from releasing consultants' reports exposing serious flaws in its own plan for London Underground; but Kiley still has original job as Mayor Ken Livingstone's transportation chief, as well as overwhelming support of London's harried straphangers; at issue is complex $18.5 billion partial privatization arrangement that government wants to put in place; plan has had little input from people experienced in running trains; photo (M)

2001-07-29.title: Powell Disappoints Critics of Arrests
2001-07-29.abstract: Family and lawyers for Liu Yaping, seriously ill businessman detained in Inner Mongolia, are disappointed that Secretary of State Colin L Powell did not raise his case during meetings with high Chinese government officials; Liu, Chinese citizen with US residency, was detained Mar 8 in Inner Mongolia in business dispute (S)

2001-07-29.title: For Nations Traumatized By the Past, New Remedies
2001-07-29.abstract: International Center for Transitional Justice has been formed to help countries come to terms with oppressions that mark their recent past; many governments and human rights groups in Asia, South America, Africa and Europe are asking for advice, information and technical assistance; center seeks to help countries emerging from state-sponsored terrorism or civil war (M)

2001-07-29.title: The Bondage of Poverty That Produces Chocolate
2001-07-29.abstract: Ivory Coast's cocoa plantations said to employ child laborers recruited or smuggled from other African countries; line between slave trading and bondage of poverty is sometimes unclear, and child labor retains deep roots in West and Central Africa; parents send their children away to earn money or learn trade; but reports of widespread abuse in Ivory Coast, world's top cocoa exporter, have generated accusations and anger in Europe and United States; in Britain, groups try to organize consumer boycott of chocolate; in Washington, House passes bill calling for voluntary system of labeling which would identify origin of cocoa in chocolate and state 'no child slave labor'; photos; map (L)

2001-07-29.title: Fabio Taglioni, Ducati Motorcycle Wizard, Dies at 80
2001-07-29.abstract: Fabio Taglioni, engineer and designer who transformed Ducati motorcycle, dies at age 80; photo (M)

2001-07-30.title: U.S. Scholar Convicted as Spy in China Returns to Hong Kong
2001-07-30.abstract: Li Shaomin, a Chinese-born American scholar convicted by Beijing of spying for Taiwan and deported to the United States last week, returned to Hong Kong today

2001-07-30.title: Bertie Felstead, 106, Soldier Who Joined a Timeout in War
2001-07-30.abstract: Bertie Felstead, the last known surviving member of the British battalion in World War I that laid down its weapons to play soccer with the Germans in a spontaneous and long-remembered Christmas truce of 1915, died July 22 in Gloucester, England. He was 106.    The truce lasted perhaps half an hour and it meant nothing in the grand schemes of the Western Front generals. But the gesture by Private Felstead's Royal Welch Fusiliers and the Bavarian infantrymen who faced them resonates in the British consciousness as a poignant interlude of civility during a time of  unrelenting carnage.

2001-07-30.title: Powell Upbeat About Relations After a Visit With Chinese
2001-07-30.abstract: The images of Secretary of State Colin L. Powell and Chinese leaders beaming at each other in their meetings this weekend, and a rare interview with the secretary broadcast today by Chinese state television, illustrate just how quickly the roller coaster relationship between the United States and China can turn.     Only three months ago, the two countries were snarling at each other over a downed American spy plane. But the message conveyed by all the bonhomie on Saturday was that, for the moment at least, those in the Bush administration who want to work with China are in the ascendancy.

2001-07-30.title: China Ferrets Out North Korean Refugees
2001-07-30.abstract: The census takers move from house to house, asking pointed questions in Chinese, demanding responses, looking for signs of incomprehension on the faces of those they see lurking in the shadows.    ''If you don't understand them, they will think you do not belong, and ask for identification,'' said a worker at a church that ministers to the Korean-Chinese community that dominates the population of this border town on the Tumen River facing North Korea. ''They will arrest you and send you back to North Korea.''

2001-07-30.title: New Rule of Law in Thailand May Be a Leader's Downfall
2001-07-30.abstract: In Thailand's slow-motion revolution from the rule of men to the rule of law, the test ahead could not be more stark.    In the coming weeks, an independent 15-member Constitutional Court is set to decide whether to remove from office the most popular Thai leader in recent times, Prime Minister Thaksin Shinawatra, based on purely a provision of law.

2001-07-31.title: White House Says the U.S. Is Not a Loner, Just Choosy
2001-07-31.abstract: Pres Bush's advisers, reacting to foreign criticisms of his opposition to some longstanding international treaties and protocols, deny he is unilateralist or isolationist, or that administration has blanket disdain for group action as way of conducting world affairs; say administration is pursuing policy of evaluating international agreements and meetings on case-by-case basis, and that treaties will be adopted when they clearly carve American interests in stone; Bush, by knocking off several high-profile treaties on arms control and environment, has been subjected to outrage from some of America's closest friends as well as adversaries, who see adoption of finger-in-the-eye confrontation in place of diplomacy; administration's opposition to several international agreements discussed; some accords it supports noted (M)

2001-07-31.title: Russians Treat Chechen Children To a Summer Vacation From War
2001-07-31.abstract: Bolshaya Yelnya, Russian village near city of Nizhny Novogorod, is summer host to children from war-torn Chechnya; children have been brought to city for third time in year through combined efforts of human rights groups and regional government; city is center of quiet protest against two Chechen wars, in which 250 soldiers from region have died; map; photo (M)

2001-07-31.title: Annan Says Race Conference Must Chart Way for Future
2001-07-31.abstract: UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan tells National Urban League world conference against racism, scheduled for Durban, South Africa, in August, that conference is aimed at preventing future problems, not becoming mired in old ones; seems to support stance of Bush administration, which has warned it may boycott UN-sponsored event because agenda includes discussion on viewing Zionism as racism and on demands for compensationn for slavery; says issues have opened up 'deep fissures' and asks organizers of conference to reach understanding that will encourage all nations to participate; photo (M)

2001-07-31.title: Reporter's Notebook; Two Mates Clown a Bit for the Aussies
2001-07-31.abstract: Reporter's Notebook column offering vignettes of Sec of State Colin Powell's visit to Beijing and his visit to Australia with Defense Sec Donald Rumsfeld; photo (M)

2001-07-31.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Corruption Arrests
2001-07-31.abstract: Officials at Shanghai's state-owned Jinjiang Group say Chen Yanning, head of asset management department, has been charged with corruption (S)
